segments.title: Cuba and the Kresta reassessment
segments.summary: The Cuban Missile Crisis exposes Soviet submarine and blue-water shortfalls, helping explain the shift from the Kynda missile cruiser to the Kresta designs.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke connects halted Kynda construction and the abandoned Shaddock-armed anti-carrier ship to a reassessment of what Soviet surface forces needed to do. Kresta I and II add helicopter facilities, stronger air defence, and anti-submarine capability, pointing toward a fleet that could operate farther from land-based aviation.

evidence.note: The opening contrasts halted Kynda construction with the Kresta I and II changes in anti-aircraft, helicopter, and long-range ASW capability.
The transcript links the Cuban Missile Crisis to Soviet submarine weaknesses and describes organic aviation, long-range air defence, and ASW as blue-water fleet requirements.

segments.title: Missile-control limits drive the next design step
segments.summary: Limited ability to control Kynda missile salvos helps explain why Soviet designers reused a proven hull while changing the weapons and roles around it.
segments.body: The video argues that a ship built around large anti-ship salvoes becomes a poor fit when its control system can guide only four missiles at once. It frames Kynda, Kresta I, and Kresta II as calculated design steps: retain the sound features, test a few changes, and let later ships reveal which capabilities deserve wider adoption.

evidence.note: The transcript says intended eight-missile salvoes were constrained by systems that could control no more than four missiles at once.
Dr. Clarke describes the classes as starting points and compares their small, calculated changes with other conservative naval design sequences.

segments.title: How Kresta I became Kresta II
segments.summary: A close comparison shows the Kresta family growing in range, anti-aircraft armament, sonar, helicopter support, and missile layout rather than changing all at once.
segments.body: The Kresta hulls lengthen and gain endurance as the series moves from four Kindas and four Kresta Is to ten Kresta IIs. The technical comparison follows the trade between anti-ship missiles, air-defence weapons, hull sonar, Ka-25 helicopters, and the fixed Silex launchers that became a recognizable Soviet cruiser feature.

evidence.note: The comparison gives displacement, length, range, missile, gun, and sonar changes across Kynda, Kresta I, and Kresta II.
The discussion covers the Kresta II's newer sonar, Ka-25 helicopter, Silex launchers, gun placement, and unfilled stern space.

segments.title: Metel gives the Kresta II reach against submarines
segments.summary: The Metel missile-and-torpedo family makes the Kresta II more than a conventional escort by extending its anti-submarine reach and creating a disruptive anti-surface option.
segments.body: The episode follows Metel from nuclear depth-charge and torpedo versions to later missile systems with helicopter, radio-command, and infrared guidance elements. Dr. Clarke stresses that the weapons are comparatively blunt instruments, yet they could complicate an opponent's task-group planning as well as hunt submarines.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the 60R and 70R versions, their payloads, ranges, torpedo options, and helicopter-supported guidance.
Dr. Clarke explains why NATO could label the ships as ASW vessels while noting the disruption a weapon-fired torpedo could cause to a task group.

segments.title: Kresta II in North Atlantic ASW operations
segments.summary: Kresta II service history shows the class performing rescue, surveillance, patrol, and anti-submarine work in the North Atlantic rather than simply displaying missiles in port.
segments.body: One ship is described assisting the damaged submarine K-19 alongside a Moskva-class helicopter carrier and other support vessels, then later searching for NATO submarines in the Norwegian Sea. These missions put the class's sonar, helicopters, weapons, and range into the wider operational pattern of Cold War naval presence and surveillance.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts Kresta participation in the 1972 rescue of K-19 with a Moskva-class helicopter carrier and other ships.
It describes a 1973 Norwegian Sea exercise searching for NATO submarines and the first Northern Fleet-area SS-N-14 launch.

segments.title: Storm air defence and the Kresta II sensor fit
segments.summary: The Storm, or NATO Goblet, missile system illustrates how the Kresta II combined large air-defence missiles with radar and fire-control equipment.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke outlines the missile's size, speed, warhead, engagement ranges, radio-command guidance, and the radar set that directed it. The arrival of the associated equipment in Kresta II ships is another clue that Soviet designers were pairing new weapons with the sensors needed to use them effectively.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Storm/Goblet missile's twin launchers, speed, mass, warhead, range, radio-command guidance, and radar equipment.
Dr. Clarke notes that the relevant radar equipment arrives with Kresta II ships and treats that as evidence of a planned system fit.

segments.title: The retractable Kresta II helicopter hangar
segments.summary: Kresta II's unusual retractable hangar preserves the existing stern and missile arrangement, but at the cost of an elaborate aircraft-handling mechanism.
segments.body: Rather than raise the hangar and rearrange the after surface-to-air missile fit, the design lifts the flight-deck section so a helicopter can be lowered below deck level. The discussion treats it as an ingenious but revealing compromise: a naval aviation feature shaped by a strong preference to retain the rest of the ship's layout.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the raised flight deck, pop-up roof, opening doors, and lowering system used to stow the helicopter.
Dr. Clarke calls the solution inventive while arguing that it follows from avoiding larger changes to the ship's existing design.

segments.title: Ka-25 helicopters and the cruiser as a presence ship
segments.summary: The Ka-25 gives Kresta II an organic anti-submarine aircraft, while the class's striking armament also serves Soviet presence, command, and deterrence missions.
segments.body: The Ka-25 Hormone began as an anti-submarine helicopter with radar, dipping sonar, a towed detector, torpedoes, and depth-charge options, making the ship's elaborate hangar operationally meaningful. The closing argument places Krestas in patrol, diplomatic, command, and deterrence roles: ships intended to contribute to task forces while visibly projecting Soviet naval capability abroad.

evidence.note: The transcript traces the Ka-25's service entry and describes its anti-submarine radar, dipping sonar, detector, and weapons.
Dr. Clarke describes patrol, presence, diplomatic, command, deterrence, and task-force roles before summarizing the Krestas as capable ships for their setting.
